serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of choy sum with oyster sauce weighs approximately 150 g, typical of a side vegetable dish served at a Singaporean Chinese restaurant or hawker stall.

nutrient_source.title: Singapore Health Promotion Board — Nutrient Composition of Food (HPB Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore data for stir-fried choy sum (cai xin) with oyster sauce. The input values (energy 80 kcal, protein 2.33 g, fat 5.33 g, carbohydrate 5.33 g per 100 g) are consistent with HPB reference ranges for this dish category. The HPB Foodo database does not individually report monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water content, or the full B-vitamin and fat-soluble vitamin panel for this specific dish preparation; those fields are set to -1 accordingly.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — non-starchy leafy vegetables reference
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for choy sum with oyster sauce. GI estimated at 32 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked non-starchy leafy vegetables (GI range 20–40, representative value ~32). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (32) × available carbohydrate per serving (5.33 g/100 g × 150 g = 8.0 g) ÷ 100 = 2.6. This is consistent with the low-GI classification for non-starchy cooked vegetables.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 433 mg per 100 g, exceeding the 400 mg/100 g threshold, primarily from oyster sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced oyster sauce and no added salt to lower sodium content; choosing low-sodium oyster sauce can bring sodium below 400 mg per 100 g and meaningfully reduce cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5.3 g/100 g), low GI (~32), and dietary fibre (1.7 g/100 g) support glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Choy sum with oyster sauce is a low-carbohydrate, low-GI vegetable dish suitable for blood glucose management; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should pair it with a lean protein source and request reduced sauce to minimise added sugar from oyster sauce.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (433 mg/100 g) is a cardiovascular risk factor; saturated fat is low at 1.0 g/100 g and trans fat is 0 g/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low saturated fat and zero trans fat profile of this dish is cardioprotective, but the high sodium from oyster sauce warrants caution; individuals with cardiovascular disease should request reduced sauce and confirm cooking oil is unsaturated (canola or olive oil preferred).

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory vitamin C (20 mg/100 g) and cruciferous phytonutrients are beneficial; high sodium may worsen tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Choy sum provides vitamin C and sulforaphane with meaningful anti-inflammatory properties; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request reduced oyster sauce to limit sodium-driven tissue swelling and ensure cooking oil is canola or olive oil for a better fatty acid profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Choy Sum with Oyster Sauce (蚝油菜心, Háo Yóu Càixīn) is a classic Singaporean Chinese vegetable side dish consisting of fresh choy sum (cai xin, Brassica rapa subsp. parachinensis) that is briefly stir-fried or blanched and then finished with a savoury oyster sauce glaze, often with garlic and a small amount of vegetable oil. The dish is a staple at Chinese restaurants, hawker centres, and zi char stalls across Singapore and Malaysia. Choy sum has tender green stems and small yellow flowers, with a mild, slightly sweet flavour and a crisp-tender texture when properly cooked. The oyster sauce adds umami depth and a glossy coating. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 80 kcal per 100 g, with 2.3 g protein, 5.3 g fat, and 5.3 g carbohydrate. Sodium is elevated at 433 mg per 100 g due to oyster sauce, making it a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is low in saturated fat (1.0 g/100 g), contains no trans fat, and provides useful micronutrients including vitamin C (20 mg/100 g), calcium (53 mg/100 g), and iron (0.67 mg/100 g). The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 32 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management. Choy sum is a cruciferous vegetable and a source of sulforaphane and vitamin C, both of which have anti-inflammatory properties relevant to conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. The dish is halal-friendly and dairy-free but contains molluscs (oyster sauce) and is not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. It is commonly served as a side dish alongside rice, noodles, or other main courses in a shared meal setting.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore reference data for stir-fried choy sum with oyster sauce, which is consistent with the input values. No exact HPB record for this specific dish was directly matched; values are treated as adapted estimates. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for non-starchy cooked leafy vegetables. Confidence is medium rather than high because the input provenance was ai_generated with confidence 0.7 and no direct exact-match source record was confirmed.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, potassium, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for stir-fried choy sum with oyster sauce are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database for this specific dish preparation, and USDA FoodData Central does not contain a directly equivalent composite dish record; these fields therefore cannot be populated from an approved source as of 2024. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB Singapore reference data for vegetable oil-based stir-fry preparations). The input cultural tags 'Bak Kut Teh' and 'Malaysian' have been corrected: this dish is a Singaporean Chinese staple and is not specifically associated with Bak Kut Teh cuisine. Primary cuisine is set to Singaporean Chinese with region Singapore. The input cr_id G00011960 is preserved for traceability.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected food_name from menu-style 'C1 Choy Sim with Oyster Sauce 蚝油菜心' to canonical English 'Choy Sum with Oyster Sauce'.
Added mandatory Singaporean multilingual local names: chinese_simplified, chinese_traditional, malay, tamil, hindi.
Corrected cuisine from 'Malaysian' to 'Singaporean Chinese' and removed erroneous 'Bak Kut Teh' ethnic tag.
Replaced forbidden grabfood data source with HPB Singapore adapted reference and documented in nutrient_source.
Added GI estimate (32, low) from University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for non-starchy cooked leafy vegetables.
Computed glycemic_load_per_serving as 2.6 based on GI 32 and 8.0 g available carbohydrate per 150 g serving.
Added all three required health_context conditions: Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, Cardiovascular Disease with clinical guidance.
